text: Adding heavy resistance and plyometric training to endurance training improves maximal strength, running economy, and peak velocity in well-trained runners without negatively affecting VO2max.
Adding endurance-strength training (40% load) to endurance training improves maximal strength, running economy, and peak velocity in well-trained runners without negatively affecting VO2max.
Heavy resistance plus plyometric training appears more effective than endurance-strength training at improving 3-km time trial performance in well-trained runners.
A concurrent training program of 6 endurance sessions and 2 strength sessions per week over 12 weeks is effective for improving running performance in well-trained runners.
Concurrent strength and endurance training does not interfere with VO2max or VO2 kinetics in well-trained runners over a 12-week period.

General strength training using resistance bands alone (without heavy resistance or plyometrics) does not produce the same performance improvements as structured concurrent strength training in well-trained runners.
